title: Lesson 6: Time Words, Telling Time & Daily Schedules   د وخت کلمې، ساعت ویل او ورځنۍ مهالویش
description: Master temporal reference by learning lexical time expressions, the 24-hour official clock convention, and navigating Afghan daily routines structured around cultural and Islamic prayer times.
duration: Textbook pp. 81–90 (approx. 10-15 pages of primary instruction plus Workbook exercises)

content.heading: Module 1: Time Words & Days of the Week
lines: Welcome to Unit 6! As you transition into Intermediate-Low proficiency, we shift our focus from static descriptions to navigating time.

Let's start with times of the day (presented with transliteration for introduction only): [səhār] (سهار) 'morning', [mazmāshi] (غرمه/مزماښي) 'midday', [māspar] (ماسپښین) 'afternoon', [māxam] (ماښام) 'evening', [shpa] (شپه) 'night'.

Key temporal adverbs: [nna] (نن) 'now/today', [mākhkhe] (مخکې) 'before/earlier', [wróste] (وروسته) 'after/later', [biyā] (بیا) 'again/then', [her waxṭ] (هر وخت) 'always', [kāla kāla] (کله کله) 'sometimes', [hēč kāla] (هیڅکله) 'never'.
Days of the week follow a numeric pattern added to 'shamba' (Saturday): [dushamba] (دوشنبه - Monday), [seshamba] (سه شنبه - Tuesday)... leading to the holy day of rest: [jomā] (جمعه - Friday).

content.heading: Module 2: The Pashto Clock System
lines: Telling time in Pashto relies on hour-noun constructions combined with prepositional directional phrases to indicate quarter-past or quarter-to.

Note the distinction between systems: In informal conversation, a 12-hour system with time-of-day markers is used. In official Afghan contexts (government, broadcasting, transportation), a strict 24-hour clock is utilized.

content.heading: ⏱️ Interactive Clock Reading
lines: [Interactive Clock Diagram]: Explore the interactive clock face below. Note: The labels on this diagram are exclusively in Pashto script, in accordance with your learning progression.
When reading clock time, accurate stress on the word [baje] and the number forms is critical.
[Audio Player]: Listen to the monosyllabic pronunciation of [nim] (half) and [rob] (quarter), contrasting them with the two-syllable number forms.

content.text: Phonetics Hack: Watch out for minimal-pair potential between [māxam] (evening) and [māspar] (afternoon). Mispronouncing these can completely change your scheduled meeting time!

content.heading: Module 3: Daily Schedules & Sentence Structures
lines: Let's build time-telling sentences using our SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) framework.
ساعت درې بجې دي. [Sāat ṭəlo baje di.] (It is three o'clock.)
ماسپښین نیمه بجه په کور کې یم. [Māspar nim baje pa kor ke yəm.] (At half past twelve I am at home. Note: 12:30 PM literally translated involves 'afternoon half o'clock').
[Interactive Drill Matrix]: Practice combining different hour markers with times of day in complete SOV sentences.

content.text: Cultural Context: The Afghan day is deeply structured by Islamic prayer times: Fajr (dawn), Dhuhr (midday), Asr (afternoon), Maghrib (sunset), and Isha (night). These are frequently used as authentic temporal references rather than clock times. Additionally, Friday ([jomā]) is the holy day and weekend, which is critical for scheduling. You will also encounter 'nīm-rōz' — the cultural practice of resting or napping after the midday meal.

content.question: If you are scheduling a business meeting in Kabul and the counterpart suggests meeting on '[jomā]', what should you consider?
options: It is the first day of the official work week.
It refers to the late afternoon nap time, so you should be quiet.
It is Friday, the Islamic holy day and traditional day of rest, so a business meeting is likely inappropriate.
It means you should strictly use the 24-hour clock to set the time.
content.explanation: In Afghanistan and Islamic culture, [jomā] (Friday) is the day of congregational prayer and the primary weekend day, making it an inappropriate day for standard business scheduling.

content.question: Which of the following time-related words are phonologically monosyllabic and require careful stress differentiation when speaking?
content.explanation: The words [nim] (half) and [rob] (quarter) are monosyllabic. Phonetic accuracy requires you to contrast these sharply with multisyllabic numbers and time words.
